subject: Chemistry
question_html: What is the basic structural difference between starch and cellulose ?
question_text: What is the basic structural difference between starch and cellulose ?
explanation: Starch: It is a branched chain polymer of α-glucose and consists of two components: Amylose(water soluble) and Amylopectin (water insoluble). Cellulose: It is a straight chain polysaccharide composed only of β-D-glucose units which are joined by glycosidic linkage between C 1 of one glucose unit and C 4 of the next glucose unit.
solution: Starch: It is a branched chain polymer of α-glucose and consists of two components: Amylose(water soluble) and Amylopectin (water insoluble). Cellulose: It is a straight chain polysaccharide composed only of β-D-glucose units which are joined by glycosidic linkage between C 1 of one glucose unit and C 4 of the next glucose unit.
solution_html:
Starch: It is a branched chain polymer of α-glucose and consists of two components: Amylose(water soluble) and Amylopectin (water insoluble).
Cellulose: It is a straight chain polysaccharide composed only of β-D-glucose units which are joined by glycosidic linkage between C1 of one glucose unit and C4 of the next glucose unit.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Why do amino acids show amphoteric behaviour?
  2. What happens when D-Glucose is treated with hydroxylamine?
question_text: Why do amino acids show amphoteric behaviour? What happens when D-Glucose is treated with hydroxylamine?
explanation: Due to the presence of both acidic (-COOH) and basic (-NH 2 ) groups in the same molecule/they form zwitter ion which react with both acid and base. {tex}\begin{array}{ccc}\overset{\overset{CHO}\vert}{\underset{\underset{CH_2OH}\vert}{{(CHOH)}_4}}&\xrightarrow{NH_2OH}&\overset{\overset{CH=N-OH}\vert}{\underset{\underset{CH_2OH}\vert}{CHOH_4}}\end{array}{/tex} /Glucose oxime is formed.
solution: Due to the presence of both acidic (-COOH) and basic (-NH 2 ) groups in the same molecule/they form zwitter ion which react with both acid and base. {tex}\begin{array}{ccc}\overset{\overset{CHO}\vert}{\underset{\underset{CH_2OH}\vert}{{(CHOH)}_4}}&\xrightarrow{NH_2OH}&\overset{\overset{CH=N-OH}\vert}{\underset{\underset{CH_2OH}\vert}{CHOH_4}}\end{array}{/tex} /Glucose oxime is formed.
solution_html:
1. Due to the presence of both acidic (-COOH) and basic (-NH2) groups in the same molecule/they form zwitter ion which react with both acid and base.
  2. {tex}\begin{array}{ccc}\overset{\overset{CHO}\vert}{\underset{\underset{CH_2OH}\vert}{{(CHOH)}_4}}&\xrightarrow{NH_2OH}&\overset{\overset{CH=N-OH}\vert}{\underset{\underset{CH_2OH}\vert}{CHOH_4}}\end{array}{/tex} /Glucose oxime is formed.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Differentiate between:

  1. Peptide linkage and Glycosidic linkage
  2. Nucleoside and Nucleotide
question_text: Differentiate between: Peptide linkage and Glycosidic linkage Nucleoside and Nucleotide
explanation: Peptide linkage: A linkage formed when two amino acids are joined through -CONH- bond. Glycosidic linkage: When two monosaccharides are joined through oxygen atom. Nucleoside: Base + Sugar Nucleotide: Base + Sugar + Phosphate
solution: Peptide linkage: A linkage formed when two amino acids are joined through -CONH- bond. Glycosidic linkage: When two monosaccharides are joined through oxygen atom. Nucleoside: Base + Sugar Nucleotide: Base + Sugar + Phosphate
solution_html:
1. Peptide linkage: A linkage formed when two amino acids are joined through -CONH- bond.
    Glycosidic linkage: When two monosaccharides are joined through oxygen atom.
  2. Nucleoside: Base + Sugar
    Nucleotide: Base + Sugar + Phosphate

question_html:
Define the following terms:

  1. Denaturation of protein
  2. Invert sugar
question_text: Define the following terms: Denaturation of protein Invert sugar
explanation: When a protein in its native form, is subjected to physical change like change in pH, temperature etc it loses its biological activity. (Or destruction of secondary and tertiary structure.) Hydrolysis of sucrose brings about a change in the sign of rotation, from dextro (+) to laevo (−) and the product is named an invert sugar.
solution: When a protein in its native form, is subjected to physical change like change in pH, temperature etc it loses its biological activity. (Or destruction of secondary and tertiary structure.) Hydrolysis of sucrose brings about a change in the sign of rotation, from dextro (+) to laevo (−) and the product is named an invert sugar.
solution_html:
1. When a protein in its native form, is subjected to physical change like change in pH, temperature etc it loses its biological activity. (Or destruction of secondary and tertiary structure.)
  2. Hydrolysis of sucrose brings about a change in the sign of rotation, from dextro (+) to laevo (−) and the product is named an invert sugar.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Why cannot vitamin C be stored in our body?
question_text: Why cannot vitamin C be stored in our body?
explanation: Vitamin C cannot be stored in our body because it is water soluble. As a result, it is readily excreted in the urine.
solution: Vitamin C cannot be stored in our body because it is water soluble. As a result, it is readily excreted in the urine.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: The two strands in DNA are not identical but are complementary. Explain.
question_text: The two strands in DNA are not identical but are complementary. Explain.
explanation: In the helical structure of DNA, the two strands are held together by hydrogen bonds between specific pairs of bases. Cytosine forms hydrogen bond with guanine, while adenine forms hydrogen bond with thymine. As a result, the two strands are complementary to each other.
solution: In the helical structure of DNA, the two strands are held together by hydrogen bonds between specific pairs of bases. Cytosine forms hydrogen bond with guanine, while adenine forms hydrogen bond with thymine. As a result, the two strands are complementary to each other.
solution_html: In the helical structure of DNA, the two strands are held together by hydrogen bonds between specific pairs of bases. Cytosine forms hydrogen bond with guanine, while adenine forms hydrogen bond with thymine. As a result, the two strands are complementary to each other.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Name any two reactions of glucose which cannot be explained by its chain structure.
  2. What is the difference between reducing and non-reducing sugars?
  3. What are Polysaccharides?
question_text: Name any two reactions of glucose which cannot be explained by its chain structure. What is the difference between reducing and non-reducing sugars? What are Polysaccharides?
explanation: Glucose does not give 2,4-DNP test. Glucose does not give Schiff's test. The pentaacetate of glucose does not react with hydroxylamine. Reducing sugars: These are carbohydrates that can act as reducing agents due to the presence of free aldehyde groups of free ketone groups. Non-reducing sugars: These are carbohydrates that cannot act as a reducing agents due to the presence of free aldehyde groups of free ketone groups.
solution: Glucose does not give 2,4-DNP test. Glucose does not give Schiff's test. The pentaacetate of glucose does not react with hydroxylamine. Reducing sugars: These are carbohydrates that can act as reducing agents due to the presence of free aldehyde groups of free ketone groups. Non-reducing sugars: These are carbohydrates that cannot act as a reducing agents due to the presence of free aldehyde groups of free ketone groups.
solution_html:
1. Glucose does not give 2,4-DNP test.
    2. Glucose does not give Schiff's test.
    3. The pentaacetate of glucose does not react with hydroxylamine.
    1. Reducing sugars: These are carbohydrates that can act as reducing agents due to the presence of free aldehyde groups of free ketone groups.
    2. Non-reducing sugars: These are carbohydrates that cannot act as a reducing agents due to the presence of free aldehyde groups of free ketone groups.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Write the important structural and functional differences between DNA and RNA.
question_text: Write the important structural and functional differences between DNA and RNA.
explanation: The structural differences between DNA and RNA are as follows: DNA RNA 1. The sugar moiety in DNA molecules is {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-2 deoxyribose. 1. The sugar moiety in RNA molecules is {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-ribose. 2. DNA contains thymine (T). It does not contain uracil (U). 2. RNA contains uracil (U). It does not contain thymine (T). 3. The helical structure of DNA is double-stranded. 3. The helical structure of RNA is single-stranded. The functional differences between DNA and RNA are as follows: DNA RNA 1. DNA is the chemical basis of heredity. 1. RNA is not responsible for heredity. 2. DNA molecules do not synthesise proteins, but transfer coded message for the synthesis of proteins in the cells. 2. Proteins are synthesised by RNA molecules in the cells.
solution: The structural differences between DNA and RNA are as follows: DNA RNA 1. The sugar moiety in DNA molecules is {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-2 deoxyribose. 1. The sugar moiety in RNA molecules is {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-ribose. 2. DNA contains thymine (T). It does not contain uracil (U). 2. RNA contains uracil (U). It does not contain thymine (T). 3. The helical structure of DNA is double-stranded. 3. The helical structure of RNA is single-stranded. The functional differences between DNA and RNA are as follows: DNA RNA 1. DNA is the chemical basis of heredity. 1. RNA is not responsible for heredity. 2. DNA molecules do not synthesise proteins, but transfer coded message for the synthesis of proteins in the cells. 2. Proteins are synthesised by RNA molecules in the cells.
solution_html:
The structural differences between DNA and RNA are as follows:

DNA

RNA

1. The sugar moiety in DNA molecules is {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-2 deoxyribose.

1. The sugar moiety in RNA molecules is {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-ribose.

2. DNA contains thymine (T). It does not contain uracil (U).

2. RNA contains uracil (U). It does not contain thymine (T).

3. The helical structure of DNA is double-stranded.

3. The helical structure of RNA is single-stranded.


The functional differences between DNA and RNA are as follows:

DNA

RNA

1. DNA is the chemical basis of heredity.

1. RNA is not responsible for heredity.

2. DNA molecules do not synthesise proteins, but transfer coded message for the synthesis of proteins in the cells.

2. Proteins are synthesised by RNA molecules in the cells.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Explain the terms primary and secondary structure of proteins. What is the difference between {tex}\alpha{/tex}-helix and {tex}\beta{/tex}-pleated sheet structure of proteins?
question_text: Explain the terms primary and secondary structure of proteins. What is the difference between {tex}\alpha{/tex} -helix and {tex}\beta{/tex} -pleated sheet structure of proteins?
explanation: Primary structure: Proteins may have one or more polypeptide chains. Each polypeptide chain has a large number of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -amino acids linked to one another in a specific sequence. The specific sequence in which the various {tex}\alpha{/tex} -amino acids present in a protein are linked to one another is called its primary structure. Any change in this primary structure, i.e. a sequence of amino acids creates a different protein. Secondary structure : The secondary structure refers to the shape in which a long polypeptide chain can exist. Therefore, it gives the shape or conformation of the protein molecule This arises from regular folding of the backbone of a peptide bond with a hydrogen bond between the > C = 0 and N-H groups of different peptide bonds {tex}\alpha{/tex} -Helix structure: a-helix form is the most common form in which a polypeptide chain forms all possible types of hydrogen bonds by twisting into a right-handed screw (helix) with the -NH group of each amino acid residue hydrogen-bonded to -C = 0 group of the adjacent turn of the helix. The {tex}\alpha{/tex} -helix structure is also known as 3.613 helix. This represents that each turn of the helix contains approximately 3.6 amino acids and a 13 -member ring is formed by hydrogen bonding. The helix is held in its shape primarily by hydrogen bonds between one amide group and carbonyl group which is 3.6 amino acids units away {tex}\beta{/tex} -pleated sheet structure: In this conformation, the polypeptide chains lie side by side in a zigzag manner with alternate R groups on the same side situated at fixed distances apart. The two such neighboring polypeptide chains are held together by intermolecular H-bonds. A number of such chains can be interblended to form a sheet. These sheets are then stacked one above the other like the pages of this book to form a three-dimensional structure. This structure resembles pleated folds of drapery and hence is also called {tex}\beta{/tex} -pleated sheet structure.
solution: Primary structure: Proteins may have one or more polypeptide chains. Each polypeptide chain has a large number of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -amino acids linked to one another in a specific sequence. The specific sequence in which the various {tex}\alpha{/tex} -amino acids present in a protein are linked to one another is called its primary structure. Any change in this primary structure, i.e. a sequence of amino acids creates a different protein. Secondary structure : The secondary structure refers to the shape in which a long polypeptide chain can exist. Therefore, it gives the shape or conformation of the protein molecule This arises from regular folding of the backbone of a peptide bond with a hydrogen bond between the > C = 0 and N-H groups of different peptide bonds {tex}\alpha{/tex} -Helix structure: a-helix form is the most common form in which a polypeptide chain forms all possible types of hydrogen bonds by twisting into a right-handed screw (helix) with the -NH group of each amino acid residue hydrogen-bonded to -C = 0 group of the adjacent turn of the helix. The {tex}\alpha{/tex} -helix structure is also known as 3.613 helix. This represents that each turn of the helix contains approximately 3.6 amino acids and a 13 -member ring is formed by hydrogen bonding. The helix is held in its shape primarily by hydrogen bonds between one amide group and carbonyl group which is 3.6 amino acids units away {tex}\beta{/tex} -pleated sheet structure: In this conformation, the polypeptide chains lie side by side in a zigzag manner with alternate R groups on the same side situated at fixed distances apart. The two such neighboring polypeptide chains are held together by intermolecular H-bonds. A number of such chains can be interblended to form a sheet. These sheets are then stacked one above the other like the pages of this book to form a three-dimensional structure. This structure resembles pleated folds of drapery and hence is also called {tex}\beta{/tex} -pleated sheet structure.
solution_html:
Primary structure: Proteins may have one or more polypeptide chains. Each polypeptide chain has a large number of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-amino acids linked to one another in a specific sequence. The specific sequence in which the various {tex}\alpha{/tex}-amino acids present in a protein are linked to one another is called its primary structure. Any change in this primary structure, i.e. a sequence of amino acids creates a different protein.

Secondary structure: The secondary structure refers to the shape in which a long polypeptide chain can exist. Therefore, it gives the shape or conformation of the protein molecule This arises from regular folding of the backbone of a peptide bond with a hydrogen bond between the > C = 0 and N-H groups of different peptide bonds
{tex}\alpha{/tex}-Helix structure: a-helix form is the most common form in which a polypeptide chain forms all possible types of hydrogen bonds by twisting into a right-handed screw (helix) with the -NH group of each amino acid residue hydrogen-bonded to -C = 0 group of the adjacent turn of the helix. The {tex}\alpha{/tex}-helix structure is also known as 3.613 helix. This represents that each turn of the helix contains approximately 3.6 amino acids and a 13 -member ring is formed by hydrogen bonding. The helix is held in its shape primarily by hydrogen bonds between one amide group and carbonyl group which is 3.6 amino acids units away

{tex}\beta{/tex}-pleated sheet structure: In this conformation, the polypeptide chains lie side by side in a zigzag manner with alternate R groups on the same side situated at fixed distances apart. The two such neighboring polypeptide chains are held together by intermolecular H-bonds. A number of such chains can be interblended to form a sheet. These sheets are then stacked one above the other like the pages of this book to form a three-dimensional structure. This structure resembles pleated folds of drapery and hence is also called {tex}\beta{/tex}-pleated sheet structure.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: What is the basic structural difference between starch and cellulose?
question_text: What is the basic structural difference between starch and cellulose?
explanation: Starch consists of two components - amylose and amylopectin. Amylose is a long linear chain of {tex}\propto{/tex} -D-(+)-glucose units joined by C 1 - C 4 glycosidic linkage ( {tex}\propto{/tex} -link). Amylopectin is a branched-chain polymer of {tex}\propto{/tex} -D-glucose units, in which the chain is formed by ​​​​​​​C 1 - C 4 glycosidic linkage and the branching occurs by {tex}{C_1} - {C_4}{/tex} glycosidic linkage. On the other hand, cellulose is a straight-chain polysaccharide of {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-glucose units joined by ​​​​​​​C 1 - C 4 glycosidic linkage ( {tex}\beta{/tex} -link).
solution: Starch consists of two components - amylose and amylopectin. Amylose is a long linear chain of {tex}\propto{/tex} -D-(+)-glucose units joined by C 1 - C 4 glycosidic linkage ( {tex}\propto{/tex} -link). Amylopectin is a branched-chain polymer of {tex}\propto{/tex} -D-glucose units, in which the chain is formed by ​​​​​​​C 1 - C 4 glycosidic linkage and the branching occurs by {tex}{C_1} - {C_4}{/tex} glycosidic linkage. On the other hand, cellulose is a straight-chain polysaccharide of {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-glucose units joined by ​​​​​​​C 1 - C 4 glycosidic linkage ( {tex}\beta{/tex} -link).
solution_html:
Starch consists of two components - amylose and amylopectin. Amylose is a long linear chain of {tex}\propto{/tex} -D-(+)-glucose units joined by C1 - C4 glycosidic linkage ( {tex}\propto{/tex} -link).

Amylopectin is a branched-chain polymer of {tex}\propto{/tex} -D-glucose units, in which the chain is formed by ​​​​​​​C1 - C4 glycosidic linkage and the branching occurs by {tex}{C_1} - {C_4}{/tex} glycosidic linkage.

On the other hand, cellulose is a straight-chain polysaccharide of {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-glucose units joined by ​​​​​​​C1 - C4 glycosidic linkage ({tex}\beta{/tex} -link).

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
On the basis of which evidences D-glucose was assigned the following structure?
{tex}\begin{array}{c}\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm{HO}\;\;\;\\{(\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm{HOH})}_4\\{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{OH}\end{array}{/tex}
question_text: On the basis of which evidences D-glucose was assigned the following structure? {tex}\begin{array}{c}\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm{HO}\;\;\;\\{(\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm{HOH})}_4\\{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{OH}\end{array}{/tex}
explanation: This structure was assigned on the basis of the following evidences: Molecular formula: C 6 H 12 O 6 is molecular formula of glucose. Straight chain structure: When aqueous solution of glucose is treated with sodium amalgam (Na/Hg) or sodium borohydride, it is reduced to sorbitol (or glucitol) a hexahydric alcohol. Prolonged heating with hydroiodic acid and red phosphorus at 100°C gives a mixture of n-hexane and 2-iodohexane. The formation of n-hexane suggests that all the six carbon atoms in glucose are arranged in a straight chain structure. Presence of five -OH groups: On acetylation with acetic anhydride, glucose gives a pentaacetate. This confirms that glucose contains five -OH groups. We know that the presence of two or more -OH groups on the same carbon atom makes the molecules unstable. Since glucose is a stable compound, therefore, the five -OH groups must be present on different carbon atoms. Presence of one primary alcoholic group: On oxidation with conc. HNO 3 , both glucose and gluconic acid give the same dicarboxylic acid and saccharic acid. The primary alcoholic group (-CH 2 OH) is always present at the end of the carbon chain. Presence of an aldehyde (-CHO) group: Glucose reacts with hydroxylamine, NH 2 OH to form oxime. This suggests that glucose contains a carbonyl (>C=O) group. On the basis of above observations, the following open CH 2 OH chain structure for glucose can be written as follows: {tex}\begin{array}{c}\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm{HO}\;\;\;\\{(\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm{HOH})}_4\\{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{OH}\end{array}{/tex}
solution: This structure was assigned on the basis of the following evidences: Molecular formula: C 6 H 12 O 6 is molecular formula of glucose. Straight chain structure: When aqueous solution of glucose is treated with sodium amalgam (Na/Hg) or sodium borohydride, it is reduced to sorbitol (or glucitol) a hexahydric alcohol. Prolonged heating with hydroiodic acid and red phosphorus at 100°C gives a mixture of n-hexane and 2-iodohexane. The formation of n-hexane suggests that all the six carbon atoms in glucose are arranged in a straight chain structure. Presence of five -OH groups: On acetylation with acetic anhydride, glucose gives a pentaacetate. This confirms that glucose contains five -OH groups. We know that the presence of two or more -OH groups on the same carbon atom makes the molecules unstable. Since glucose is a stable compound, therefore, the five -OH groups must be present on different carbon atoms. Presence of one primary alcoholic group: On oxidation with conc. HNO 3 , both glucose and gluconic acid give the same dicarboxylic acid and saccharic acid. The primary alcoholic group (-CH 2 OH) is always present at the end of the carbon chain. Presence of an aldehyde (-CHO) group: Glucose reacts with hydroxylamine, NH 2 OH to form oxime. This suggests that glucose contains a carbonyl (>C=O) group. On the basis of above observations, the following open CH 2 OH chain structure for glucose can be written as follows: {tex}\begin{array}{c}\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm{HO}\;\;\;\\{(\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm{HOH})}_4\\{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{OH}\end{array}{/tex}
solution_html:
This structure was assigned on the basis of the following evidences:

  1. Molecular formula: C6H12O6 is molecular formula of glucose.
  2. Straight chain structure:
    1. When aqueous solution of glucose is treated with sodium amalgam (Na/Hg) or sodium  borohydride, it is reduced to sorbitol (or glucitol) a hexahydric alcohol.
    2. Prolonged heating with hydroiodic acid and red phosphorus at 100°C gives a mixture of n-hexane and 2-iodohexane.

      The formation of n-hexane suggests that all the six carbon atoms in glucose are arranged in a straight chain structure.
    3. Presence of five -OH groups: On acetylation with acetic anhydride, glucose gives a pentaacetate. This confirms that glucose contains five -OH groups. We know that the presence of two or more -OH groups on the same carbon atom makes the molecules unstable.
      Since glucose is a stable compound, therefore, the five -OH groups must be present on different carbon atoms.
    4. Presence of one primary alcoholic group: On oxidation with conc. HNO3, both glucose and gluconic acid give the same dicarboxylic acid and saccharic acid. The primary alcoholic group (-CH2OH) is always present at the end of the carbon chain.
    5. Presence of an aldehyde (-CHO) group: Glucose reacts with hydroxylamine, NH2OH to form oxime. This suggests that glucose contains a carbonyl (>C=O) group.
      On the basis of above observations, the following open CH2OH chain structure for glucose can be written as follows:
      {tex}\begin{array}{c}\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm{HO}\;\;\;\\{(\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm{HOH})}_4\\{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{OH}\end{array}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. How do you explain the amphoteric behaviour of amino acids?
  2. Classify the following into monosaccharides and disaccharides: ribose, 2-deoxyribose, maltose, galactose, fructose and lactose.
question_text: How do you explain the amphoteric behaviour of amino acids? Classify the following into monosaccharides and disaccharides: ribose, 2-deoxyribose, maltose, galactose, fructose and lactose.
explanation: In aqueous solution, the carboxyl group of an amino acid can lose a proton and the amino group can accept a proton to give a dipolar ion known as zwitter ion. {tex}\mathrm R-\underset{:{\mathrm{NH}}_2}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm O-\mathrm H\rightleftharpoons\mathrm R-\underset{\mathrm{Zwitter}\;\mathrm{ion}}{\underset{{}^+\mathrm{NH}_3}{\mathrm C\underset\vert{\mathrm H}-}\mathrm C\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}-}}\mathrm O^-{/tex} Therefore, in zwitter ion form, the amino acid can act both as an acid and as a base. In the acidic medium, COO - ion of the zwitter ion accepts a proton to form the cation, and in the basic medium, + NH 3 ion loses a proton to form the anion, as shown below: {tex}\mathrm R-\underset{{\mathrm{NH}}_2}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm O^-\overset{\mathrm H^+}{\underset{\mathrm{OH}^-}\rightleftharpoons}\mathrm R-\underset{{}^+\mathrm{NH}_3}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm O^-\overset{\mathrm H^+}{\underset{\mathrm{OH}^-}\rightleftharpoons}\mathrm R-\underset{{}^+\mathrm{NH}_3}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm{OH}{/tex} Thus, amino acids show amphoteric behaviour. Monosaccharides: Ribose and fructose. Disaccharides: Maltose and lactose.
solution: In aqueous solution, the carboxyl group of an amino acid can lose a proton and the amino group can accept a proton to give a dipolar ion known as zwitter ion. {tex}\mathrm R-\underset{:{\mathrm{NH}}_2}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm O-\mathrm H\rightleftharpoons\mathrm R-\underset{\mathrm{Zwitter}\;\mathrm{ion}}{\underset{{}^+\mathrm{NH}_3}{\mathrm C\underset\vert{\mathrm H}-}\mathrm C\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}-}}\mathrm O^-{/tex} Therefore, in zwitter ion form, the amino acid can act both as an acid and as a base. In the acidic medium, COO - ion of the zwitter ion accepts a proton to form the cation, and in the basic medium, + NH 3 ion loses a proton to form the anion, as shown below: {tex}\mathrm R-\underset{{\mathrm{NH}}_2}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm O^-\overset{\mathrm H^+}{\underset{\mathrm{OH}^-}\rightleftharpoons}\mathrm R-\underset{{}^+\mathrm{NH}_3}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm O^-\overset{\mathrm H^+}{\underset{\mathrm{OH}^-}\rightleftharpoons}\mathrm R-\underset{{}^+\mathrm{NH}_3}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm{OH}{/tex} Thus, amino acids show amphoteric behaviour. Monosaccharides: Ribose and fructose. Disaccharides: Maltose and lactose.
solution_html:
1. In aqueous solution, the carboxyl group of an amino acid can lose a proton and the amino group can accept a proton to give a dipolar ion known as zwitter ion.
    {tex}\mathrm R-\underset{:{\mathrm{NH}}_2}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm O-\mathrm H\rightleftharpoons\mathrm R-\underset{\mathrm{Zwitter}\;\mathrm{ion}}{\underset{{}^+\mathrm{NH}_3}{\mathrm C\underset\vert{\mathrm H}-}\mathrm C\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}-}}\mathrm O^-{/tex}
    Therefore, in zwitter ion form, the amino acid can act both as an acid and as a base. In the acidic medium, COO- ion of the zwitter ion accepts a proton to form the cation, and in the basic medium, +NH3 ion loses a proton to form the anion, as shown below:
    {tex}\mathrm R-\underset{{\mathrm{NH}}_2}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm O^-\overset{\mathrm H^+}{\underset{\mathrm{OH}^-}\rightleftharpoons}\mathrm R-\underset{{}^+\mathrm{NH}_3}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm O^-\overset{\mathrm H^+}{\underset{\mathrm{OH}^-}\rightleftharpoons}\mathrm R-\underset{{}^+\mathrm{NH}_3}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm{OH}{/tex}
    Thus, amino acids show amphoteric behaviour.
  2. Monosaccharides: Ribose and fructose.
  3. Disaccharides: Maltose and lactose.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: How do you explain the absence of aldehyde group in the pentaacetate of D-glucose?
question_text: How do you explain the absence of aldehyde group in the pentaacetate of D-glucose?
explanation: D-glucose reacts with hydroxylamine NH 2 OH to form an oxime because of the presence of aldehydic (-CHO) group or carbonyl carbon. This happens as the cyclic structure of glucose forms an open chain structure in an aqueous medium, which then reacts with NH 2 OH to give an oxime. But pentaacetate of D-glucose does not react with NH 2 OH. This is because pentaacetate does not form an open chain structure.
solution: D-glucose reacts with hydroxylamine NH 2 OH to form an oxime because of the presence of aldehydic (-CHO) group or carbonyl carbon. This happens as the cyclic structure of glucose forms an open chain structure in an aqueous medium, which then reacts with NH 2 OH to give an oxime. But pentaacetate of D-glucose does not react with NH 2 OH. This is because pentaacetate does not form an open chain structure.
solution_html:
D-glucose reacts with hydroxylamine NH2OH to form an oxime because of the presence of aldehydic (-CHO) group or carbonyl carbon. This happens as the cyclic structure of glucose forms an open chain structure in an aqueous medium, which then reacts with NH2OH to give an oxime.

But pentaacetate of D-glucose does not react with NH2OH. This is because pentaacetate does not form an open chain structure.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Write the structures of fragments produced on the complete hydrolysis of DNA. How are they linked in DNA molecule? Draw a diagram to show the pairing of nucleotide bases in the double helix of DNA.
question_text: Write the structures of fragments produced on the complete hydrolysis of DNA. How are they linked in DNA molecule? Draw a diagram to show the pairing of nucleotide bases in the double helix of DNA.
explanation: Complete hydrolysis of the DNA yields a pentose sugar, phosphoric acid, and nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compounds called bases. In DNA molecules, the sugar moiety is B-D-2-deoxyribose. Structures: Sugar: Phosphoric acid: Nitrogen base: DNA contains four bases viz, Adenine (A), Guanine (G), Cytosine (C) and Thymine (T). A unit formed by the attachment of a base to I'-position of sugar is called nucleoside. When nucleoside links to phosphoric acid at 5 -position of the sugar moiety, a nucleotide is formed. Nucleotides are joined together by phosphodiester linkage between 5 '-and 3 'carbon atoms of the pentose sugar. In DNA, two chains of the nucleic acid coil about each other and held together by H-bonds between bases of two chains.
solution: Complete hydrolysis of the DNA yields a pentose sugar, phosphoric acid, and nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compounds called bases. In DNA molecules, the sugar moiety is B-D-2-deoxyribose. Structures: Sugar: Phosphoric acid: Nitrogen base: DNA contains four bases viz, Adenine (A), Guanine (G), Cytosine (C) and Thymine (T). A unit formed by the attachment of a base to I'-position of sugar is called nucleoside. When nucleoside links to phosphoric acid at 5 -position of the sugar moiety, a nucleotide is formed. Nucleotides are joined together by phosphodiester linkage between 5 '-and 3 'carbon atoms of the pentose sugar. In DNA, two chains of the nucleic acid coil about each other and held together by H-bonds between bases of two chains.
solution_html:
Complete hydrolysis of the DNA yields a pentose sugar, phosphoric acid, and nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compounds called bases. In DNA molecules, the sugar moiety is B-D-2-deoxyribose.
Structures:

  1. Sugar: 
  2. Phosphoric acid:
  3. Nitrogen base: DNA contains four bases viz, Adenine (A), Guanine (G), Cytosine (C) and Thymine (T).

    A unit formed by the attachment of a base to I'-position of sugar is called nucleoside. When nucleoside links to phosphoric acid at 5 -position of the sugar moiety, a nucleotide is formed. Nucleotides are joined together by phosphodiester linkage between 5 '-and 3 'carbon atoms of the pentose sugar.

    In DNA, two chains of the nucleic acid coil about each other and held together by H-bonds between bases of two chains.

question_html:
1. Answer the following question briefly:
    1. What are any two food sources of vitamin A?
    2. What are nucleotides?
  2. How are carbohydrate classified?
question_text: Answer the following question briefly: What are any two food sources of vitamin A? What are nucleotides? How are carbohydrate classified?
explanation: Carrot and cod liver oil. Nucleotides are monomers of nucleic acids. They consist of heterocyclic base, pentose sugar and phosphoric acid reside. Carbohydrate are classified as: Monosacchrides Oligosaccharides Polysacchrides.
solution: Carrot and cod liver oil. Nucleotides are monomers of nucleic acids. They consist of heterocyclic base, pentose sugar and phosphoric acid reside. Carbohydrate are classified as: Monosacchrides Oligosaccharides Polysacchrides.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Proteins are the most abundant biomolecules of the living system. Proteins are the polymers of about twenty different {tex}\alpha{/tex}-amino acids which are linked by peptide bonds. Ten amino acids are called essential amino acids. In zwitter ionic form, amino acids show amphoteric behaviour as they react both with acids and bases.
On the basis of their molecular shape, proteins are classified into two types: Fibrous and Globular proteins. Structure and shape of proteins can be studied at four different levels i.e., primary, secondary, tertiary and quaternary, each level being more complex than the previous one. The secondary or tertiary structure of proteins get disturbed on change of pH or temperature and they are not able to perform their functions. This is called denaturation of proteins.
Answer the following questions:

  1. What are essential amino acids? (1)
  2. What is meant by zwitter ionic form of amino acids? (1)
    1. Give one example each for Fibrous protein and Globular protein.
    2. What type of linkages hold monomers of proteins together? (2{tex}\times{/tex}1 = 2)

      OR

    1. What is the structural  feature which characterises a reducing sugar?
    2. What is the structural difference between nucleoside and nucleotide? (2{tex}\times{/tex}1 = 2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Proteins are the most abundant biomolecules of the living system. Proteins are the polymers of about twenty different {tex}\alpha{/tex} -amino acids which are linked by peptide bonds. Ten amino acids are called essential amino acids. In zwitter ionic form, amino acids show amphoteric behaviour as they react both with acids and bases. On the basis of their molecular shape, proteins are classified into two types: Fibrous and Globular proteins. Structure and shape of proteins can be studied at four different levels i.e., primary, secondary, tertiary and quaternary, each level being more complex than the previous one. The secondary or tertiary structure of proteins get disturbed on change of pH or temperature and they are not able to perform their functions. This is called denaturation of proteins. Answer the following questions: What are essential amino acids? (1) What is meant by zwitter ionic form of amino acids? (1) Give one example each for Fibrous protein and Globular protein. What type of linkages hold monomers of proteins together? (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 2) OR What is the structural feature which characterises a reducing sugar? What is the structural difference between nucleoside and nucleotide? (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 2)
explanation: Amino acids which cannot be synthesised in the body and must be obtained through diet. Dipolar ion formation /lon having both anionic and cationic parts / {tex}\mathrm R-\underset{{}^+{\mathrm{NH}}_3}{\underset\vert{\mathrm{CH}}}-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm O^-{/tex} Keratin, myosin (any one) - Fibrous Albumin, Insulin (any one) - Globular Peptide linkage / structure OR Free aldehydic or ketonic group/ Aldehydic (or carbonyl) group is not involved in glycosidic linkage. In nucleotide, phosphate group is attached to 5'-position of sugar moiety while phosphate group is absent in nucleoside / Nucleoside consist of a molecule of sugar linked to nitrogenous base Nucleotide consist of a molecule of sugar, nitrogenous base and phosphoric acid
solution: Amino acids which cannot be synthesised in the body and must be obtained through diet. Dipolar ion formation /lon having both anionic and cationic parts / {tex}\mathrm R-\underset{{}^+{\mathrm{NH}}_3}{\underset\vert{\mathrm{CH}}}-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm O^-{/tex} Keratin, myosin (any one) - Fibrous Albumin, Insulin (any one) - Globular Peptide linkage / structure OR Free aldehydic or ketonic group/ Aldehydic (or carbonyl) group is not involved in glycosidic linkage. In nucleotide, phosphate group is attached to 5'-position of sugar moiety while phosphate group is absent in nucleoside / Nucleoside consist of a molecule of sugar linked to nitrogenous base Nucleotide consist of a molecule of sugar, nitrogenous base and phosphoric acid
solution_html:
1. Amino acids which cannot be synthesised in the body and must be obtained through diet.
  2. Dipolar ion formation /lon having both anionic and cationic parts / {tex}\mathrm R-\underset{{}^+{\mathrm{NH}}_3}{\underset\vert{\mathrm{CH}}}-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-\mathrm O^-{/tex}
    1. Keratin, myosin (any one) - Fibrous
      Albumin, Insulin (any one) - Globular
    2. Peptide linkage / structure

      OR

    1. Free aldehydic or ketonic group/ Aldehydic (or carbonyl) group is not involved in glycosidic linkage.
    2. In nucleotide, phosphate group is attached to 5'-position of sugar moiety while phosphate group is absent in nucleoside / Nucleoside consist of a molecule of sugar linked to nitrogenous base
      Nucleotide consist of a molecule of sugar, nitrogenous base and phosphoric acid

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Carbohydrates are essential for life in both plants and animals. Carbohydrates are used as storage molecules as starch in plants and glycogen in animals. Chemically they are polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones. On the basis of their behaviour on hydrolysis, carbohydrates are classified as monosaccharides, oligosaccharides and polysaccharides. All monosaccharides are reducing sugars, i.e., they are oxidized by Tollens' reagent and Fehling's solution. A monosaccharide like glucose is aldohexose and its molecular formula was found to be C6H12O6. After reacting with different reagents like HI, H2N - OH, Bromine water, (CH3CO)2O, etc. its structure was found to contain one aldehyde group, one primary alcoholic group, (-CH2OH) and four secondary alcoholic
groups (> CHOH). Despite having the aldehyde group, glucose does not give some of the reactions of aldehyde group like Schiff's test, NaHSO3 addition. This explains the existence of glucose in two cyclic hemiacetal forms which differ only in the configuration of the hydroxyl group at C-1.
Answer the following questions:

  1. What are reducing sugars? (1)
  2. Classify the following into monosaccharide and disaccharide:
    Fructose, Sucrose, Lactose, Galactose (1)
  3. Name the polysaccharide which is known as 'animal starch'. Why is it called 'animal starch'? (2)

    OR

  1. Name the isomers of glucose which in the cyclic form differ only in the configuration of the -OH group at C-1.
  2. Presence of which functional group was detected when glucose reacted with Br2 water? (2{tex}\times{/tex}1 = 2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Carbohydrates are essential for life in both plants and animals. Carbohydrates are used as storage molecules as starch in plants and glycogen in animals. Chemically they are polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones. On the basis of their behaviour on hydrolysis, carbohydrates are classified as monosaccharides, oligosaccharides and polysaccharides. All monosaccharides are reducing sugars, i.e., they are oxidized by Tollens' reagent and Fehling's solution. A monosaccharide like glucose is aldohexose and its molecular formula was found to be C 6 H 12 O 6 . After reacting with different reagents like HI, H 2 N - OH, Bromine water, (CH 3 CO) 2 O, etc. its structure was found to contain one aldehyde group, one primary alcoholic group, (-CH 2 OH) and four secondary alcoholic groups (> CHOH). Despite having the aldehyde group, glucose does not give some of the reactions of aldehyde group like Schiff's test, NaHSO 3 addition. This explains the existence of glucose in two cyclic hemiacetal forms which differ only in the configuration of the hydroxyl group at C-1. Answer the following questions: What are reducing sugars? (1) Classify the following into monosaccharide and disaccharide: Fructose, Sucrose, Lactose, Galactose (1) Name the polysaccharide which is known as 'animal starch'. Why is it called 'animal starch'? (2) OR Name the isomers of glucose which in the cyclic form differ only in the configuration of the -OH group at C-1. Presence of which functional group was detected when glucose reacted with Br 2 water? (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 2)
explanation: Sugars which reduce Tollens' reagent or Fehling's solution. Monosaccharides:- Fructose, Galactose Disaccharides:- Sucrose, lactose Glycogen, because its structure is similar to amylopectin. OR Anomers / {tex}\alpha{/tex} -D-Glucose and {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-Glucose Aldehyde /-CHO group
solution: Sugars which reduce Tollens' reagent or Fehling's solution. Monosaccharides:- Fructose, Galactose Disaccharides:- Sucrose, lactose Glycogen, because its structure is similar to amylopectin. OR Anomers / {tex}\alpha{/tex} -D-Glucose and {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-Glucose Aldehyde /-CHO group
solution_html:
1. Sugars which reduce Tollens' reagent or Fehling's solution.
  2. Monosaccharides:- Fructose, Galactose
    Disaccharides:- Sucrose, lactose
  3. Glycogen, because its structure is similar to amylopectin.

    OR

  1. Anomers / {tex}\alpha{/tex}-D-Glucose and {tex}\beta{/tex}-D-Glucose
  2. Aldehyde /-CHO group

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Carbohydrates are the major components of all living organisms. Sugars are carbohydrates. The major types of sugars include monosaccharides and disaccharides. The main difference between monosaccharides, disaccharides and polysaccharides is that monosaccharides are monomer of sugars and disaccharides are composed of two monomers, whereas polysaccharides are composed of a large number of monomers. Monosaccharides are single sugar molecules which act as the building blocks of disaccharides and polysaccharides. Disaccharides are also simple sugars. Disaccharides are classified into two groups according to their reducing strength : Reducing and Non-reducing sugars. When a polymer is formed from a monomer, a condensation reaction occurs that forms a glycosidic bond and water molecule is lost. Starch, glycogen and cellulose are examples of polysaccharides. Starch is found in many parts of plant cell and consists of amylose and amylopectin. Glycogen is the major carbohydrate storage product found in humans. It is present in liver, muscles and brain.
Cellulose is the most abundant organic molecule on Earth. It makes up around 50% of all organic carbon.

  1. Name the linkage which connects monosaccharide units in polysaccharides. (1)
  2. Carbohydrates are classified on the basis of their behaviour on hydrolysis. Write the hydrolysis products of sucrose. (1)
  3. Write two differences between Amylose and Amylopectin. (2)
    OR
    1. What are reducing sugars? (2)
    2. Sucrose is dextrorotatory but the mixture obtained after hydrolysis is laevorotatory. Why?
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Carbohydrates are the major components of all living organisms. Sugars are carbohydrates. The major types of sugars include monosaccharides and disaccharides. The main difference between monosaccharides, disaccharides and polysaccharides is that monosaccharides are monomer of sugars and disaccharides are composed of two monomers, whereas polysaccharides are composed of a large number of monomers. Monosaccharides are single sugar molecules which act as the building blocks of disaccharides and polysaccharides. Disaccharides are also simple sugars. Disaccharides are classified into two groups according to their reducing strength : Reducing and Non-reducing sugars. When a polymer is formed from a monomer, a condensation reaction occurs that forms a glycosidic bond and water molecule is lost. Starch, glycogen and cellulose are examples of polysaccharides. Starch is found in many parts of plant cell and consists of amylose and amylopectin. Glycogen is the major carbohydrate storage product found in humans. It is present in liver, muscles and brain. Cellulose is the most abundant organic molecule on Earth. It makes up around 50% of all organic carbon. Name the linkage which connects monosaccharide units in polysaccharides. (1) Carbohydrates are classified on the basis of their behaviour on hydrolysis. Write the hydrolysis products of sucrose. (1) Write two differences between Amylose and Amylopectin. (2) OR What are reducing sugars? (2) Sucrose is dextrorotatory but the mixture obtained after hydrolysis is laevorotatory. Why?
explanation: Glycosidic linkage D-(+)-Glucose and D-(-)-fructose Amylose Amylopectin Soluble in water Insoluble in water Linear long chain polymer Branched chain polymer OR Sugars which reduce Fehling's / Tollen's reagents. Hydrolysis of sucrose gives dextrorotatory glucose and laevorotatory fructose. But the laevorotation of fructose is more than the dextrorotation of glucose. Hence, the mixture is laevorotatory.
solution: Glycosidic linkage D-(+)-Glucose and D-(-)-fructose Amylose Amylopectin Soluble in water Insoluble in water Linear long chain polymer Branched chain polymer OR Sugars which reduce Fehling's / Tollen's reagents. Hydrolysis of sucrose gives dextrorotatory glucose and laevorotatory fructose. But the laevorotation of fructose is more than the dextrorotation of glucose. Hence, the mixture is laevorotatory.
solution_html:
1. Glycosidic linkage
  2. D-(+)-Glucose and D-(-)-fructose
  3. Amylose Amylopectin
    Soluble in water Insoluble in water
    Linear long chain polymer Branched chain polymer
    OR
    1. Sugars which reduce Fehling's / Tollen's reagents.
    2. Hydrolysis of sucrose gives dextrorotatory glucose and laevorotatory fructose. But the laevorotation of fructose is more than the dextrorotation of glucose. Hence, the mixture is laevorotatory.
